mnemonics: FILE Descriptors Are Keys (FDAK): Think of a file descriptor as a key that unlocks a dictionary (file) for reading. You need the right key (fd) to access the right book (file).
keyPoints: Use the `open()` system call to get a file descriptor for the dictionary file.
Use the `read()` system call within a loop to read chunks of data from the file descriptor into a buffer.
Always check the return value of system calls for errors (e.g., `open()` returns -1, `read()` returns -1) and handle them appropriately.
Remember to properly manage memory for your read buffer and to close the file descriptor with `close()` when finished.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to check the return value of `open()` for errors, leading to attempts to read from an invalid file descriptor.
Not handling the return value of `read()` correctly, especially the 0 which indicates End-of-File (EOF).
Using standard I/O functions (like `fread`) when the topic specifically requires using the low-level system calls (`open`, `read`, `close`).
